name: Analfabeto
classification_notes: The typeface is styled as rustic lettering with a vernacular, hand-crafted character, placing it firmly in the display category with a folk or naive subcategory.
summary: A rustic display typeface by Flavio Morais and Joan Barjau, paired with a companion pictographic set.
description: Analfabeto draws on vernacular and hand-lettered traditions to produce a rough, informal display face. It is accompanied by Analfabeto Pics, a pictographic companion font, making the family useful for decorative and craft-influenced work. Best suited to packaging, posters or branding projects that call for a naive, hand-made quality.
foundry_blurb: Type-ø-tones present Analfabeto as a rustic lettering typeface designed by Flavio Morais and Joan Barjau, available in a text style and a pictographic variant called Analfabeto Pics.

name: Arboria
classification_notes: The even stroke weights, clean forms and geometric naming conventions suggest a geometric sans-serif tradition, sitting alongside mid-century European models.
summary: A geometric sans-serif by José Manuel Urós offered in six weights with matching italics, suited to editorial and corporate applications.
description: Designed by José Manuel Urós and published by Type-ø-tones, Arboria covers six weights from Thin to Black, each paired with an italic. The even stroke construction and clean letterforms follow a geometric sans tradition, making it adaptable across print and screen contexts. The full family of twelve styles gives sufficient range for multi-weight typographic systems.
foundry_blurb: Arboria is a sans-serif typeface by José Manuel Urós, available in six weights from Thin to Black with corresponding italics, making up a twelve-style family.

name: Adelita
classification_notes: The family includes variants named Fina, Ole, Topos and Dibujitos, suggesting a playful, illustrative and decorative display typeface rather than a functional text face.
summary: A playful display family by Adela de Bara and Laura Meseguer, offered in four expressive stylistic variants.
description: Adelita comes in four distinct sub-families: Fina, Ole, Topos and Dibujitos, each suggesting a different visual register from delicate to illustrative. The naming and character of the variants point to a Spanish-inflected sensibility, likely suited to branding, editorial headlines and packaging. Designed collaboratively by Adela de Bara and Laura Meseguer under the Type-ø-tones imprint.
foundry_blurb: Type-ø-tones present Adelita as a multi-variant display family by Adela de Bara and Laura Meseguer, available in four styles: Fina, Ole, Topos and Dibujitos, each sold individually or as a complete family.

name: Arbotek
classification_notes: The rounded and thin weight variants, along with the clean naming conventions, point to a geometric sans tradition; the presence of a rounded cut further confirms this classification.
summary: A geometric sans by José Manuel Urós available in four weights including a rounded light cut, sold through Type-ø-tones.

The Light Rounded variant gives the family a softer register while the Ultra provides contrast for display use. The architectural references in the specimen text suggest positioning for editorial or cultural institution work.
foundry_blurb: Arbotek is a geometric sans-serif family by José Manuel Urós, offered in four styles including a rounded variant, available as individual weights or as a complete family.

name: Blak
classification_notes: The name, the comic-book specimen text, and the ultra-weight cuts all point to a blackletter display family; the slanted variants and extreme ultra weight reinforce its use as a display face.

The specimen leans into comic-book and superhero references, suggesting the design targets editorial and entertainment contexts where bold blackletter registers drama. The slanted cuts act as pseudo-italics, adding momentum without a full italic construction.
foundry_blurb: Blak is a blackletter display typeface by Íñigo Jerez, available in four styles: Regular, Slanted, Ultra and Ultra Slanted.

name: ASM
classification_notes: The page offers no explicit classification, but the specimen text and style names suggest a straightforward grotesque sans-serif with upright and italic cuts across regular and bold weights.

The UD variant likely denotes an upright or alternate design direction, though no further detail is provided on the page. A clean, functional grotesque suited to editorial and display use.
foundry_blurb: Type-ø-tones offer ASM by Íñigo Jerez as a complete family of eight fonts, covering Regular and Bold weights with matching italics across two subfamilies: ASM and ASM UD.

name: Brise

The family splits into Text and Display subfamilies, with the Display branch reaching an Extreme weight suited to headlines and poster work. A launch variable font licence is available alongside per-style purchasing options.

name: Cortada Dos

The stencil and packing variants give the family a strong industrial and printed-material register, useful for identities and editorial work that needs a technical or experimental tone. The range of styles within one family allows for varied visual expression while maintaining structural coherence.

name: Despatxada

name: DINosaur
classification_notes: DINosaur draws from the DIN tradition of geometric, engineered sans-serifs, with a name that directly references DIN while suggesting a reinterpretation of that industrial lineage.
summary: A six-weight DIN-derived geometric sans by José Manuel Urós, with matching italics across every weight.
description: Drawing on the engineered clarity of the DIN tradition, DINosaur offers six weights from Thin to Black, each paired with an italic, for a total of twelve styles. The name signals both its lineage and a looser, more contemporary take on the industrial grotesque model. Suited to editorial, wayfinding and corporate contexts where DIN-flavoured neutrality is wanted without strict adherence to the original standard.
foundry_blurb: DINosaur is a geometric sans-serif by José Manuel Urós offered in six weights — Thin, Light, Book, Medium, Bold and Black — each with a matching italic, available individually or as a complete family.

The naming nods to its DIN heritage while the sharp detailing gives it a slightly more contemporary edge.
foundry_blurb: Type-ø-tones present DINosaur Sharp as a family designed by José Manuel Urós, available in twelve styles spanning six weights with accompanying italics, and supporting Latin, Cyrillic and Greek scripts.

The approach allows designers to shift from low-contrast to high-contrast versions within the same family, suiting editorial and expressive typographic contexts. name: Eixample Villa

The specimen text draws on figures associated with urban planning and architecture, from Ildefons Cerdà to Bruno Taut, suggesting the typeface is consciously positioned within that tradition. Designed by José Manuel Urós and Sabina Chipară at Type-ø-tones.

name: Ella
classification_notes: Ella spans four distinct subfamilies — Roman, Uncial, Rustic and Brutalist — all serif-rooted display variants, suggesting a broad explorative display serif family with historical and expressive references.
summary: A TDC 2023 award-winning display serif family by Laura Meseguer, spanning four distinct stylistic variants from classical Roman to Brutalist.
description: Designed by Laura Meseguer, Ella is structured across four named subfamilies — Roman, Uncial, Rustic and Brutalist — each available in Regular and Bold, giving the family a wide expressive range within a shared serif sensibility. The grouping draws on historical lettering traditions while the Brutalist cut pushes the family into more contemporary, raw territory. Recognised by the Type Directors Club in 2023, it suits editorial and cultural projects that benefit from typographic contrast across a single type system.
foundry_blurb: Ella is a display type family by Laura Meseguer, winner of a TDC 2023 award, comprising four subfamilies — Roman, Uncial, Rustic and Brutalist — each in Regular and Bold weights, available individually or as a complete family.

classification_notes: Fabulosa appears to be a display typeface with expressive, likely script-influenced characteristics, consistent with Laura Meseguer's known output in expressive and calligraphic display type.

name: Gotta Scotch
classification_notes: The name directly references the Scotch Roman tradition, a category of transitional serifs with high contrast, robust bracketed serifs and upright stress developed in 19th-century Scottish and American type foundries.

The Scotch Roman genre brings pronounced stroke contrast, sturdy bracketed serifs and a crisp upright axis suited to editorial and display work. A focused, historically grounded family for settings that want period warmth without pastiche.

foundry_blurb: Type-ø-tones present Kanat Latin by Maria Montes, available in a single Ultra weight with a 20% launch discount. The font is offered with a standard licence and Latin script support.

name: Joost
classification_notes: A geometric sans with Bauhaus references, including a stencil variant; the naming and specimen text (Gropius, Meyer, Moholy-Nagy, Kandinsky, Bayer) explicitly signal the Bauhaus design lineage.
summary: A geometric sans by José Manuel Urós, drawn in the Bauhaus tradition and offered in four weights plus a stencil variant.
description: Joost takes its cues from the Bauhaus school, with specimen text naming Gropius, Moholy-Nagy, Kandinsky and Bayer making the lineage explicit. The family runs from Light to Bold across four weights, with a companion Stencil cut that leans into the constructivist, modular character of the design. It suits editorial, identity and display work where a Bauhaus reference is wanted without reaching for historical revivals.
foundry_blurb: Type-ø-tones present Joost as a geometric sans designed by José Manuel Urós, offered in Light, Regular, Medium and Bold alongside a Stencil variant, with Bauhaus figures cited throughout the specimen.

name: Lalola
classification_notes: Lalola is a display typeface with expressive, informal letterforms suggesting a hand-drawn or script-influenced style, suitable for headlines and short text rather than body copy.
summary: An expressive display typeface by Laura Meseguer, recognised in TDC 2014 and available in Latin and Cyrillic.
description: Designed by Laura Meseguer and released in 2013, Lalola combines informal, hand-drawn energy with enough structure for confident headline use. The family covers both Latin and Cyrillic scripts, broadening its reach for Eastern European markets. Recognition at TDC 2014 and inclusion in Typefacts' Best Fonts of 2013 mark it as a noted release of its year.
foundry_blurb: Type-ø-tones present Lalola as a display typeface by Laura Meseguer, offered in Regular with a separate Cyrillic variant, and recognised by TDC 2014 and listed among the best fonts of 2013 at Typefacts.

name: Karol
classification_notes: The name, specimen samples and style suggest a classical text serif with upright roman and italic cuts across a weight range from Regular to Black, consistent with transitional serif conventions.
summary: A classical text serif by Daniel Sabino offered in four weights with matching italics, suited to editorial and long-form setting.

The specimen samples suggest a text-oriented serif with classical proportions and a considered rhythm across weights. It sits comfortably in book, editorial and branding contexts where a legible, conventionally structured serif is required.
foundry_blurb: Type-ø-tones offer Karol as a complete family designed by Daniel Sabino, available in Regular, SemiBold, Bold and Black, each with a matching italic.

name: Karol Sans
classification_notes: The range of weights and italics, combined with the foundry's general design sensibility, points to a humanist sans genre with text-oriented intentions. No explicit classification is given on the page.
summary: A six-weight sans-serif by Daniel Sabino, each weight accompanied by a matching italic, suited to text and editorial work.
description: Karol Sans by Daniel Sabino spans six weights from Light to Black, with a full set of italic companions across every weight. The family totals twelve styles, offering a practical range for continuous text and editorial applications. Released by the Barcelona-based Type-ø-tones foundry, it sits in a humanist sans tradition with clear text-setting utility.

name: Magothic

Available in a single Regular weight, it is positioned as a playful or conceptual piece rather than a workhorse family. The foundry's own description — 'really, it's a clever joke' — signals an irreverent intent typical of Type-ø-tones' experimental output.
foundry_blurb: Type-ø-tones describe Magothic simply as 'really, it's a clever joke', a single-weight display font designed by Enric Jardí.

name: Magasin

The family sits alongside other Meseguer scripts such as The Sweety, Confitura and Moderna.

name: Matricia
classification_notes: Matricia is styled after dot matrix printer output, with subfamilies named after printer models and escape codes, placing it firmly in the display/bitmap-revival category.
summary: A dot matrix printer revival family by José Manuel Urós and Pera Ribalta, with each variant referencing specific printer models and hardware.
description: Three subfamilies draw directly from dot matrix printer aesthetics, with Matricia Uno, Matricia Uno Extended and Matricia Dos each named after real machine references including the Honeywell 9255L6 and escape code sequences. The single weight per variant suggests a narrow, functional design brief rooted in faithful hardware reproduction rather than typographic range. Suited to retro computing, lo-fi editorial or anything that wants to evoke the texture of early digital print.
foundry_blurb: Type-ø-tones present Matricia as a dot matrix printer-inspired family, with its three variants evoking specific machines and printer codes: Matricia Uno references dot matrix output, Matricia Uno Extended the Honeywell 9255L6, and Matricia Dos the Escape Code minus 27.

name: Memimas Pro

A Dots variant adds a decorative alternative to the standard cuts, broadening its range for graphic and typographic applications. The family suits headline work, packaging and branding where a lively, expressive serif is called for.

description: Designed by Enric Jardí and published through Type-ø-tones, Mayayo is a display family built around one bold weight in three stylistic variants: Black, Holes and Inline. name: Multi

The Display subfamily spans Thin through Poster with matching italics, while the Text subfamily covers Regular through Bold. Its in-use credits include Het Parool, The New Yorker, Rolling Stone, Billboard and Wallpaper, reflecting a strong track record in demanding editorial environments.

The pairing suits poster, editorial and packaging contexts where strong contrast and decorative weight are called for. The Inline cut adds a secondary register for layered or single-colour typographic treatments.
foundry_blurb: Type-ø-tones present Neeskens as a display family by Enric Jardí, available in Black and Inline styles, offered individually or as a complete family.

name: Mundo

Mariscal's characteristic playfulness and illustrative sensibility inform the design, placing it firmly in the expressive Barcelona design tradition of the late 1980s and early 1990s. The limited weight range reflects its origins as a purpose-built display face rather than a versatile text family.

name: Optica

This approach, common in pre-digital type but less frequently pursued in contemporary digital releases, prioritises legibility and optical consistency at small text sizes. Designed by José Manuel Urós for Type-ø-tones, the family suits editorial and book work where size-specific refinements matter.

name: Poca
designer: Enric Jardí, José Manuel Urós, Pere Torrent
classification_notes: Poca is a pixel-style display typeface with named variants (Ten, Fourteen) referencing grid resolution, evoking the aesthetic of early arcade and video game typography.
summary: A pixel-style display family by Enric Jardí, José Manuel Urós and Pere Torrent, built around the grid aesthetics of early arcade video games.

The references to Space Invaders and arcade culture are worn openly, making it a deliberate period piece rather than a general-purpose typeface. Best suited to display settings where retro gaming associations are the point.
foundry_blurb: Poca is a pixel typeface by Enric Jardí, José Manuel Urós and Pere Torrent, available in three variants — Regular, Ten and Fourteen — evoking the aesthetic of classic arcade games such as Space Invaders.

name: Retorica

The family splits into two styles: Retorica Plena (full, solid forms) and Retorica Buida (empty, outline forms), giving designers a straightforward pairing for layered or standalone titling work. The three variants suggest a progression from compact to more expressive, calligraphic forms, making the family suited to editorial headlines and display contexts where rhythm and warmth matter. The Latin release sits alongside an Arabic counterpart, hinting at cross-script influences in the design.
foundry_blurb: Type-ø-tones present Qandus Latin as a nine-style display family designed by Laura Meseguer, structured across three stylistic variants, Solid, Original and Fluid, each offered in Regular, Medium and Dark weights.

name: Rothwood

name: Rumba

The calligraphic forms give it warmth and rhythm, making it well suited to food, culture and editorial contexts. Its optical size structure is an unusual and considered approach for a display family.

name: Scena

The theatrical naming of sample strings — directors, playwrights and performers — hints at the intended context: cultural institutions, programmes and stage-adjacent identity work. Slanted rather than true italic variants keep the character set rational and consistent across widths.
foundry_blurb: Scena is a sans-serif family designed by José Manuel Urós and Sabina Chipară, available in two widths — standard and Wide — each running from Thin to Black with slanted companions, sold as individual styles or as a complete family.

summary: A single-weight serif by Enric Jardí with a Catalan literary sensibility, designed at Type-ø-tones.
description: Verdaguera is a text serif from Enric Jardí at Type-ø-tones, its name evoking the Catalan poet Jacint Verdaguer and a regional literary tradition. Only a Regular weight is offered, suggesting a focus on refined text setting rather than a broad family range. Suits book, magazine or cultural publishing work where a quiet, rooted voice is preferred.
foundry_blurb: Type-ø-tones present Verdaguera as a font by Enric Jardí, with specimen text drawn from Quadern de les Idees, les Arts i les Lletres, pointing to a literary and cultural frame of reference.

name: Wilma
classification_notes: Wilma is a layered display family with multiple decorative variants including shadow, volume, pattern, fill and interior cuts, placing it firmly in the decorative display category rather than any conventional text classification.
summary: A layered display family by Enric Jardí offering nineteen variants built around shadow, volume, patterned fill and interior cut styles for chromatic typesetting.
description: Wilma is designed by Enric Jardí and released through Type-ø-tones as a system of nineteen complementary display fonts. The family is structured around a Base layer with companions for shadow (Ombra), volumetric shading (Volum A–C), patterned fills (Motiu A–C), open pattern cuts (Motiu Forat A–C), dense fills (Farcit A–B) and interior treatments (Interior A–C and Interior Forat A–C). The layering system allows designers to mix and match variants for richly textured, chromatic headline work.
foundry_blurb: Wilma is a display type family by Enric Jardí available from Type-ø-tones. The family comprises nineteen styles including Base, Ombra, Volum, Motiu, Farcit and Interior cuts, designed to be layered together for decorative and chromatic typographic compositions.

name: Xiquets
classification_notes: The three subfamilies — Primitives, Afrotibet, and Egyptmaya — suggest a decorative, culturally referential display typeface drawing on diverse historical scripts and ornamental traditions, placing it firmly in the experimental display category.
summary: A display family by Joan Barjau comprising three stylistically distinct subfamilies drawing on diverse cultural and historical visual references.
description: Three subfamilies — Primitives, Afrotibet, and Egyptmaya — each bring a distinct aesthetic rooted in cultural and historical visual traditions. The family is designed for display use where strong graphic character is required. Its unusual naming and range of references suggest an interest in cross-cultural ornamentation rather than neutral utility.
foundry_blurb: Xiquets is a display font family by Joan Barjau, available in three variants: Primitives, Afrotibet, and Egyptmaya, each sold individually or as a complete family.

name: ZooLogic
classification_notes: The name, three distinct styles and specimen presentation suggest an illustrative or novelty display typeface; insufficient detail to assign a more specific subcategory beyond display.
summary: A three-style display family by José Manuel Urós, sold individually or as a complete set at a launch discount.
description: ZooLogic comprises three distinct styles designed by José Manuel Urós and released by Type-ø-tones. Each style is priced separately at 55 euros, with a 20% launch discount applied across the family. The specimen phrases suggest a literary or editorial sensibility, though the name and multi-style structure point to an expressive, decorative character.
foundry_blurb: ZooLogic is a display typeface by José Manuel Urós, available in three individual styles that can also be purchased as a complete family. Type-ø-tones are currently offering a 20% launch discount on the release.

name: Zubizarreta
classification_notes: The three named sub-styles — Sobria, Regia, Tosca — suggest a range of contrasting serif treatments typical of a display-oriented family; the lack of weight range and ornate naming convention point toward a display serif classification.
summary: A display serif family by Joan Barjau comprising three stylistically distinct sub-styles — Sobria, Regia and Tosca.
description: Designed by Joan Barjau and published by Type-ø-tones, Zubizarreta is a display serif family split into three named sub-styles: Sobria, Regia and Tosca. Each style appears to offer a distinct typographic character rather than a weight progression, suggesting the family is intended for varied editorial or cultural applications. The naming references figures from Basque cultural history, lending the family a regional and contextual specificity.
foundry_blurb: Type-ø-tones present Zubizarreta as a type family by Joan Barjau, offered in three distinct styles — Sobria, Regia and Tosca — each available individually or as a complete family.
